metadata.title: SYNGO_BP_2442
metadata.comment:
Rationale: shRNA-knockdown of PRRT2 decreases spontaneous and evoked synaptic transmission. Decreased mEPSC frequency and amplitude were observed in autaptic hippocampal neurons expressing PRRT2-shRNA compared to control shRNA (Figure 4A-C). Release probability measured at excitatory and inhibitory synapses was impaired in knockdown neurons (FIgure 4D-E, S5). Co-expression of shRNA-resistant PRRT2 with the targeted shRNA was sufficient to rescue the decrease in EPCS amplitude and increase in PPR (Figure 4 D-E). These data support a role for PRRT2 in regulating presynaptic function.

shRNA-knockdown of PRRT2 in autaptic hippocampal neurons impaired evoked (40Hz, 2 sec) synchronous release (Figure 5). PRRT2-knockdown resulted in 4-5-fold reduction in mEPCS amplitude with no change in the number of SVs in RRP (Fig. 5C), implicating PRRT2 in Ca-dependent release. PRRT2-knockdown rendered spontaneous and evoked release insensititve to changes in extracellular calcium concentration (Figure 6B) and occluded the effect of EGTA observed in control neurons (Figure 6C).

PRRT2 interacts with the Ca-sensors synaptotagmin (Syt) 1 and Syt2 and SNARE proteins (SNAP-25, VAMP2, Figure 7A-B). Interaction demonstrated using a pull-down assay from mouse synaptosomal lysates using Flag-PRRT2 purified from HEK cells (Fig.7A) and co-immunoprecipitation of PRRT2 with Syt1 and Syt2 in mouse brain extracts (Fig. 7B).. Experimental description: Immunostaining: DIV 15 hippocampal neurons; Low-density and autaptic hippocampal neurons were prepared from C57BL6/J mice as previously described (Baldelli et al., 2007).

5/12/2017 Pim
- The species of the overexpressed/shRNA insensitive is not stated in the paper. Authors contacted for species information (fabio.benfenati@iit.it, dd. 5/12/2017). Authors answered:

"Mouse.
I thought it was specified in the Supplementaries
Fabio Benfenati"

For rescue experiments, we generated PRRT2-Cherry Sh1-res and PRRT2-Cherry Sh4-res constructs carrying
silent mutations that render them insensible to knocking-down.
SH1 resistant: GGCCACAGACCTGAGCCTGAA
SH4 resistant: CATGTGGCCAGTGAATATCGT

Generation of PRRT2 shRNAs and sh-resistant PRRT2. Based on the ORF of mouse PRRT2 transcript, 5
shRNAs were designed (Mission shRNA custom cloning, Sigma-Aldrich):
Sh1: GGCCACAGACCTCAGTTTAAA
Sh2: GAGGTCCTGACAGAAAGTACA
Sh3: CTCGGGACTATATCATCCTTG
Sh4: CATGTGGCCTGTCAACATTGT
Sh5: TGTCCAGGTTGTAGCAGAGAT
As a negative control, we used the following scrambled ShRNA sequence, based on the sequence of Sh1:
GCCGACACTCGTCAGTTTAAA. An additional control ShRNA, with a fully unrelated sequence that does
not target any known human or mouse genes (Luciferase shRNA; Sigma-Aldrich) was occasionally used with
similar results. Sequence alignment of human and mouse PRRT2 regions targeted by the five shRNAs (see
below, highlighted in yellow) revealed the presence of several mismatches with the human sequence, accounting
for the specificity of the shRNAs in knocking down the endogenous mouse PRRT2 expression.
